description: Run the first proxy comparison for the `jumprelu` family at K=128 using the prototype already added under `sparsify/`. Keep the round focused on establishing whether this family can materially improve the anchor-quality regime before any smaller-K or throughput tradeoffs.
self_review: This is the narrowest coherent follow-up because there is still no empirical signal for the new family. Risk remains runtime overhead from the dense encode path, but that should be interpreted as implementation cost unless quality is also clearly unpromising.

description: Advance the `jumprelu` family from prototype to stabilization with a single family-specific parameter sweep point at the K=128 anchor. Keep architecture and optimizer fixed and test a sharper thresholded encoder to see whether the current 0.1888 full FVU can be pushed materially lower before considering any cost tradeoffs.
self_review: This is a focused follow-up on the only family with positive evidence so far. The main risk is over-sparsifying early and hurting reconstruction, but that is the right failure mode to test next because the current signal suggests threshold behavior is the key family-specific lever.

description: Stay on the best known working JumpReLU K=128 configuration and run a single softer-gating proxy check by increasing gate bandwidth slightly, keeping the round focused on stability recovery and a clean read on threshold dynamics.
self_review: This stays within crash-recovery constraints and makes only a minor parameter adjustment on the current best working family. The main risk is that bandwidth softening may be too small to move FVU materially, but it is the safest informative step after consecutive crashes and the prior perf regression.

The main risk is that lower LR may simply slow progress without improving FVU, but it is the cleanest next exploitation step given that K=64 and K=32 both trailed the K=128 baseline.

description: Keep JumpReLU fixed at the K=128 quality anchor and continue the single-parameter LR sweep by testing 1.6e-3 next.
self_review: This follows the planned opposite-direction LR probe after 4e-4 underperformed and keeps the round tightly scoped to one parameter. The main risk is overshooting and harming training, but the previous lower-LR result strongly suggests the baseline is not simply overstepping already.

description: Keep JumpReLU fixed at the K=128 quality anchor and continue the single-parameter LR sweep by testing 1.6e-3 next. full
self_review: This follows the planned opposite-direction LR probe after 4e-4 underperformed and keeps the round tightly scoped to one parameter. The main risk is overshooting and harming training, but the previous lower-LR result strongly suggests the baseline is not simply overstepping already.

description: Keep the improved JumpReLU K=128 anchor fixed and continue the LR sweep upward to 3.2e-3 as the next single-parameter refinement.
self_review: This follows directly from the successful 1.6e-3 promotion and keeps the round tightly scoped to LR only. The main risk is overshooting and degrading training, but the last result justifies testing the next higher bracket before moving to auxk or expansion sweeps.

description: Keep the improved JumpReLU K=128 anchor fixed and continue the LR sweep upward to 3.2e-3 as the next single-parameter refinement. full

description: Hold the improved JumpReLU K=128 setup fixed at LR=1.6e-3 and start the auxk sweep by setting AUXK_ALPHA=0 to test whether pure reconstruction training beats the current anchor.
self_review: The LR sweep already found a clear improvement and 3.2e-3 only gave a marginal archive result, so shifting to auxk is the cleanest next one-variable probe. The main risk is increased dead features or weaker long-run stability, but proxy is the right stage to test whether the auxiliary term is helping or hurting frontier quality.

description: Keep the best-quality JumpReLU K=128 configuration fixed at LR=1.6e-3 and continue the auxk sweep with AUXK_ALPHA=0.01 after the zero-auxk ablation underperformed.
self_review: This follows directly from the failed AUXK_ALPHA=0 test and probes the obvious intermediate point without changing any other variable. The main risk is that auxk may simply not be the limiting factor, but 0.01 is the most informative remaining near-baseline setting before moving on to expansion-factor sweeps.

description: Keep the best K=128 JumpReLU quality anchor fixed and begin the expansion-factor sweep with EXPANSION_FACTOR=16, since the recent auxk sweep underperformed and capacity is the next clean one-parameter probe.
self_review: This follows the runtime exploitation guidance and changes exactly one primary variable after two auxk reductions both degraded proxy quality. The main risk is extra capacity may not help at fixed optimization settings, but EF=16 is the most justified next test before broader architecture changes.

description: Keep the best K=128 JumpReLU anchor fixed and continue the single-parameter expansion-factor sweep with EXPANSION_FACTOR=4 after EF=16 was only archive-level.
self_review: This follows the current exploitation plan and isolates the last untested expansion-factor setting before moving to a different parameter axis. The main risk is reduced capacity worsening reconstruction, but EF=4 is the cleanest remaining test to determine whether EF=8 is actually near-optimal.

description: Use the current best working JumpReLU K=128 setup as the stability anchor and make one minor parameter adjustment by testing AUXK_ALPHA=0.0625 in proxy.
self_review: This respects crash-recovery constraints by avoiding code edits and new architectures, and it returns to the strongest validated configuration with a single conservative parameter change. The main risk is that higher auxk may over-regularize reconstruction, but it is a safer stability-oriented probe than another architecture shift.

description: Switch from stalled JumpReLU tuning to a proxy top-k architecture run at K=128 while keeping the strongest validated training settings fixed for a clean architecture comparison.
self_review: This satisfies the requirement to try a fundamentally different architecture approach after prolonged stagnation, while avoiding the invalid gated-only overrides that caused recent crashes. The main risk is top-k underperforming JumpReLU, but it is the cleanest valid architecture pivot available without editing code.

description: Launch a proxy gated-family prototype at K=128 using the strongest validated training regime, but avoid architecture-specific override keys that previously caused invalid-env crashes.
self_review: This keeps the round focused on a real architecture change rather than another exhausted local sweep, and it directly addresses the recent gated launch failures by using only confirmed-safe env overrides. The main risk is that gated remains worse than top-k and JumpReLU, but that is the most informative remaining architecture probe available without code changes.

The main risk is that gated is not capacity-limited, but EF=16 is a cleaner stabilization probe than jumping back to minor optimizer tweaks.

description: Run the first real proxy comparison for `group_topk` at K=128 using the current strongest gated training regime, but without the unsupported `GROUP_TOPK_SIZE` env override that caused the earlier crashes.

The main risk is that hard local competition may still be too restrictive to challenge gated on FVU, but one clean proxy run is necessary before deciding whether to retire or stabilize this family.

description: Run a fresh `topk` K=128 proxy mainline comparison using the current strongest signum gated regime to see whether the improved training setup materially closes the quality gap for the incubating top-k family.
self_review: This is a better use of the round than another gated-adjacent parameter tweak because the search has gone too long without advancing a distinct family, and `topk` already has working runs that can cleanly absorb the stronger regime. The main risk is that top-k remains well behind gated on FVU, but that negative result is still useful because it would retire a lingering incumbent alternative and narrow future exploration toward more novel architectures.

description: Run a gated K=128 proxy stabilization pass at `LR=2e-4` on the current EF=16 signum baseline to test whether a lower learning rate improves FVU on the main quality anchor.
self_review: This keeps the round narrow, avoids mixing code and architecture changes, and targets the current best validated family with a plausible quality-oriented hyperparameter sweep. The main risk is undertraining in proxy, but that still gives a useful bound on whether lower-LR gated tuning is worth further attention.

description: Run a gated K=128 proxy stabilization pass with `EXPANSION_FACTOR=4` on the current signum baseline, keeping LR, AUXK, and architecture fixed.

The main risk is a clear FVU regression from reduced capacity, but that result would still be informative because it closes the current gated sweep and sharpens the case for returning to architecture exploration.
